seed: tenderness vs rage; inside_vs_outside; quiet_channel; climb; constellation against rain; pixel_bleed; saturated_vs_muted; crown; guilt_forgiveness | abstract variation request 32; target batch count 500
title: Signal vs Noise
reasoning: The emotional tone combines rage and tenderness, suggesting a struggle between raw, jagged edges and soft, nurturing spaces. The palette of red, black, pale blue, and white captures this dichotomy, with red representing rage's intensity, and pale blue and white embodying tenderness and calm. The arrangement features layered bands where the vibrant red interrupts muted blacks and soft blues to illustrate a dominant emotion, portraying tension and collision in each pixel's placement. Main contrasts occur in the center, where radial rage patterns collide with the gentler, ring-like tenderness patterns, emphasizing conflict yet creating a visual harmony through their interaction. This composition tells a story of emotional fracturing, peace amid turmoil, and the interplay of contrasting forces.

layout.name: stacked_bands
layout.description: Rows behave like emotional strata, shifting density and temperature from band to band.

composition.name: signal_vs_noise
composition.idea: One readable pulse tries to survive inside static, interference, and false echoes.

referenceExample.title: Rage Entering Calm
referenceExample.pattern: red jagged pixels cutting into blue horizontal bands

generationRules: Pick 1 pattern type for emotion A.
Pick 1 pattern type for emotion B.
Pick 1 shape seed as the central object.
Apply 1 to 6 broken pixels to create imperfection.
Leave at least 10 percent negative space unless the mood is panic, rage, urgency, euphoria, or pressure.
Use the shape seed as an abstract organizing metaphor, not as a literal icon.
Make the two selected pattern types visibly collide, overlap, interrupt, or trade space.
Prefer 4 to 8 distinct hex colors, with at least one quiet field color and one accent color.
Keep the 8x8 grid readable at thumbnail size by preserving clear large-scale structure.
Let one emotion dominate 55 to 70 percent of the square unless the selected layout demands balance.
Use broken pixels as intentional disruptions, scars, repairs, leaks, or false echoes.
Avoid perfectly even distribution unless the chosen composition is control, serenity, clarity, or certainty.
Give the main collision a location: center, edge, corner, diagonal, border, or channel.
generationSpec.negativeSpaceRule: Leave at least 10 percent negative space unless the chosen emotional tone needs dense pressure.
